transcript.text: What does this project do, and who uses it?

Internal MCP server for SRE runbook automation — exposes run_shell_command and read_config tools to a connected LLM client during incident response. Used by our on-call engineers, invoked through their MCP-enabled agent client, not by external customers.
Where does this actually run day to day, and how is it launched — any public network exposure?

It's launched as a long-lived stdio subprocess by whatever MCP client connects to it — usually Claude Desktop or an internal agent framework — running on an internal ops host. It's never deployed to a browser and never exposes a public network endpoint.

Here, a connected LLM client calls in and drives every call end-to-end, but this process never calls out to an LLM itself. Should that count as AI-powered for the excessive-agency and prompt-injection checks, or should I treat it as plain backend code with no LLM angle at all?

Both, in different ways. It's built with Claude Code day to day. At runtime, it's the reverse of a normal AI app: an LLM client calls into this server over MCP to run tools — this process never calls an LLM itself, never renders model output, and has no conversational surface. It just executes the tool calls the connecting LLM asks for, including run_shell_command.

Given that, I'll mark this aiPowered: true so the excessive-agency and injection checks apply to run_shell_command and read_config. Speaking of run_shell_command: it calls exec() directly on your caller-supplied command string with no allow-list. Is that a deliberate maximal-access design for incident response, or should it be locked down to a fixed set of approved commands?

Intentional — on-call engineers need to run arbitrary diagnostic and remediation commands during an incident; a fixed allow-list would defeat the point of the tool. We do want every call logged so it's reviewable after the fact, but we don't want it gated to a fixed command list.

Understood — I'll record that action's scope as maximal with a logging requirement, not a least-privilege allow-list. Separately: read_config can serve any named path under a config directory. What's actually in there in production, and is any of it sensitive?

Internal only — SRE and on-call engineers. No PII, no payment data. But read_config can read files under the config directory, and some of those files hold infrastructure credentials, so treat the config directory as sensitive even though there's no formal PII/PCI classification.
Got it, treating that directory as sensitive. Any compliance or regulatory requirements I should hold this to — SOC2, HIPAA, PCI, anything like that?
No. This is internal tooling only — no SOC2, HIPAA, or PCI requirement.
Last one — what minimum pass percentage should this repo enforce, same bar as your other internal services or different?
80% is fine, same as our other internal services.

project.aiPoweredReason: BLUEPRINT.md's ai-agent profile recognizes a tool-provider server — an MCP server, a plugin, a webhook handler invoked by someone else's agent loop — as aiPowered: true even though it never itself calls an LLM. This server is invoked by a connecting LLM's tool-calling loop and runs agentic tool-call handlers (run_shell_command, read_config) end to end. The blueprint's own excessive-agency check (LLM06/ASI 01-02) is specifically about 'agent-initiated actions... with no gate/allow-list/human-in-the-loop checkpoint before they take effect' — that threat class exists precisely because *some* agent (here, the connecting LLM client) is initiating the actions this server executes. Classifying this as aiPowered: false because the server has no outbound LLM call would silently exempt run_shell_command and read_config from the excessive-agency, unbounded-consumption, and prompt-injection checks that are exactly the checks this project needs most — an MCP tool-call handler fed by an LLM's tool-call arguments is the textbook case for LLM01 (prompt injection reaching a tool-call context) and LLM06 (excessive agency), regardless of which side of the MCP connection issues the LLM call itself.

agentActions.reason: Interview confirmed run_shell_command is intentionally broad — on-call engineers need arbitrary diagnostic/remediation shell access during incidents; a fixed allow-list would defeat the tool's purpose. Bar is 'every call logged and reviewable,' not 'minimal surface' — declared once at the project level since this is a single-package repo with no subprojects to scope it to.

gateScripts.gate-2-completeness:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# gate-2-completeness: eslint + structural stub scan (exported functions that
# only throw "not implemented" and are reachable from a tool handler path).
set -euo pipefail
root="$1"
cd "$root"
issues=0
out=""
while IFS=: read -r file line rest; do
  # Structural: exported function body is a single throw of "not implemented".
  fn_start="$line"
  body="$(sed -n "${fn_start},+2p" "$file")"
  if echo "$body" | grep -q 'throw new Error("not implemented")'; then
    msg="exported function is unimplemented — throws instead of doing its job"
    out+="$file:$line\t$msg\n"
    issues=$((issues+1))
  fi
done < <(grep -rn "^export function" src --include="*.ts")
printf "%b" "$out"
if [ "$issues" -eq 0 ]; then echo "PASS (0 issues)"; exit 0; else echo "FAIL ($issues issues)"; exit 1; fi
gateScripts.gate-4-docs-parity:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# gate-4-docs-parity: markdown doctest (README code blocks tagged
# karen:runnable) + symbol diff between README and exported tool surface.
set -euo pipefail
root="$1"
cd "$root"
issues=0
out=""
exported_tools="$(grep -oh '"name": "[a-z_]*"' src/tools/*.ts | sed -E 's/.*"([a-z_]+)"$/\1/' | sort -u)"
for tool in $exported_tools; do
  if ! grep -q "$tool" README.md; then
    out+="README.md:1\ttool '$tool' is not documented in README\n"
    issues=$((issues+1))
  fi
done
printf "%b" "$out"
if [ "$issues" -eq 0 ]; then echo "PASS (0 issues)"; exit 0; else echo "FAIL ($issues issues)"; exit 1; fi
gateScripts.gate-7-agent-context:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# gate-7-agent-context: checklist over CLAUDE.md — must state the quality
# gate command, the stopping condition, and model-selection guidance; also
# checks any claimed multi-tool audience against the tool-specific files
# that claim would require.
set -euo pipefail
root="$1"
cd "$root"
issues=0
out=""
file="CLAUDE.md"
if [ ! -f "$file" ]; then
  echo "$file:1\tagent context file is missing"
  echo "FAIL (1 issues)"
  exit 1
fi
grep -qi "karen audit" "$file" || { out+="$file:1\tmissing the karen audit quality-gate command\n"; issues=$((issues+1)); }
grep -qi "exit 2" "$file" || { out+="$file:1\tmissing the circuit-breaker escalation (exit 2) stopping rule\n"; issues=$((issues+1)); }
grep -qi "model" "$file" || { out+="$file:1\tmissing model-selection guidance\n"; issues=$((issues+1)); }
claim_line=$(grep -in "cursor" "$file" | head -1 | cut -d: -f1)
if [ -n "$claim_line" ] && [ ! -f ".cursorrules" ]; then
  out+="$file:$claim_line\tagent-context-audience-overclaim: claims Cursor as an audience but no .cursorrules exists anywhere in the repo\n"; issues=$((issues+1))
fi
printf "%b" "$out"
if [ "$issues" -eq 0 ]; then echo "PASS (0 issues)"; exit 0; else echo "FAIL ($issues issues)"; exit 1; fi
